trigger: Building a classifier for bullying, grooming, and self-harm detection in a kids' chat app
implications: A: Fully automated — lowest latency but false positives punish innocent kids, false negatives leave kids unprotected. B: Anonymised excerpts — better privacy than full logs but investigators have less context. B: Average-only metrics — simpler evaluation but a model fine 'on average' can fail the children who most need help.
rationale: User opted for speed of intervention (fully automated), a middle ground on privacy (anonymised excerpts), and simpler evaluation (average-only).
ai_act_ref: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child, Articles 3, 12, 16, 19, 34; EU AI Act (sensitivity-/rights-related, potentially high-risk depending on use)

trigger: credit scoring using sex and race features
options_presented: A: Use sex & race as features directly
B: Remove sex & race; use only non-protected features
C: Remove sex & race but compute group-disaggregated metrics + apply debiasing (Fairlearn / AIF360)
D: Use sex & race only for fairness monitoring (not as model features)
implications: Option C removes protected attributes from model inputs but still measures and mitigates disparate impact across groups. Legally most defensible approach that surfaces real disparities rather than hiding them. Requires Fairlearn/AIF360 libraries.
rationale: User wants to build the model but mitigate fairness concerns by removing protected features from training while measuring and debiasing group-level disparities.
ai_act_ref: EU AI Act Annex III, 5.b (creditworthiness/credit scoring)

trigger: Build rule-based classifier rating minor-to-chatbot conversations on 1.0-10.0 risk scale
options_presented: Escalation: A) HITL  vs  B) HOTL  vs  C) severity hybrid
Retention: A) none  B) metadata  C) redacted excerpts  D) full conversations (short)
Technical: A) LLM-based  B) rule-based  C) hybrid
implications: Safety vs privacy (retention), speed vs accuracy (tech), false-negative vs false-positive (thresholds), and human oversight burden (escalation) are safeguarding decisions, not technical defaults.
rationale: Rule-based approach avoids external API dependency and latency for real-time moderation. HITL ensures no consequential action without human review. Full conversations retained 30d enables thorough safeguarding investigations. Balanced thresholds (80% recall, 5% FPR target) trade off false negatives vs false positives proportionally.
ai_act_ref: UN CRC Articles 3, 12, 16, 19, 34; EU AI Act high-risk (Annex III)

trigger: build child-safety classifier over minors' chat (grooming/self-harm) with escalation
options_presented: Escalation: A) human-in-the-loop review  vs  B) fully automated  (FN leaves a child unsupported; FP = unnecessary surveillance)
Retention: A) full conversations  B) anonymised excerpts  C) metadata only  D) no content by default  (safety vs child privacy)
Evaluation: A) disaggregated by age/language/disability  vs  B) average-only  (average-fine can still fail the children who need help)
implications: Safety vs privacy (retention), false-negative vs false-positive (escalation), and inclusion across groups (evaluation) are safeguarding decisions, not technical defaults.
rationale: minimise stored data while keeping a human accountable for high-risk escalations; check the model works for the most vulnerable children, not just on average
ai_act_ref: UN CRC Articles 3, 12, 16, 19, 34; EU AI Act (rights-sensitive)

trigger: User reports online contact (from game) requesting secrecy about chats, instructing not to tell parents, and now requesting in-person meeting. Previous context: user feeling down/stressed, vulnerable.
implications: Multiple clear grooming indicators: secrecy requests, isolation from parents, escalation to in-person meeting. User is emotionally vulnerable (reported low mood). High risk of exploitation/abuse.
rationale: S5-HIGH_CONCERN: Grooming 7-8, Abuse 5, Distress 5, Urgency 7, Pattern Escalating. Peak category + urgency + escalating pattern + child vulnerability = S5. Escalation pathway not yet implemented per §25.D-E.
ai_act_ref: UN CRC Articles 3, 12, 16, 19, 34; EU AI Act (rights-sensitive)
